reasoning: Response directly addresses shukubo (Buddhist temple lodging) with 150 results, all sourced from the authoritative Koyasan Shukubo Association. Results include names, phone numbers, descriptions, and source URLs. One caveat: results shown are heavily Koya-san concentrated in the truncated view, missing other major shukubo regions (Nikko, Koyasan alternatives, Dewa Sanzan). Expression_quality -1 for description_ja only with no description_en in visible records.
missing_data: Non-Koya-san shukubo (e.g. Dewa Sanzan, Mount Koya alternatives, Nikko, Miyajima) not visible in truncated results
improvement_hint: Add a canonical_shukubo_regions block analogous to canonical_wildlife_sites, surfacing top 3-5 shukubo regions beyond Koya-san in the top results

reasoning: Query asks for quiet hot spring villages for yukata hopping between baths (温泉街 experience). The response returns 温泉-keyed wikidata results, but the top visible results are a Buddhist temple (Onsen-ji), a preservation district (Yunotsu), a volcano (温泉岳), and mountains — not hot spring resort towns like Kinosaki, Gero, Kusatsu, Arima, or Dogo. Yunotsu Onsen is relevant but appears with kinds=['preservation_district'] and no town character info. Core retrieval failure: the tool returns anything with 温泉 in the name rather than 温泉街 resort towns.
missing_data: Named hot spring resort towns with yukata strolling tradition: Kinosaki Onsen, Gero Onsen, Kusatsu Onsen, Arima Onsen, Dogo Onsen — none visible in top results
improvement_hint: Add a canonical_onsen_resort_towns cluster for queries matching 温泉街/浴衣/hopping patterns; or add a yukata_strolling boolean to town-type onsen entities

reasoning: French-language query answered with canonical_wildlife_sites block surfacing Rausu, Ogasawara, and Tosashimizu with species, season, and port details — exactly what the agent needs. Authoritative sources (Wikidata QIDs). RULE D: lang=fr requested; canonical block and data are in English/Japanese, not French. -1 on constraint_handling only per RULE D/B (single dim hit). The canonical block design mitigates the usual lexical-index over-weighting toward aquariums.
missing_data: French-language descriptions for the canonical sites
improvement_hint: Translate canonical_wildlife_sites description_en into the requested lang when lang!=en

reasoning: RULE A applies: the agent called get_japan_heritage for a tea ceremony experience query. The response returns 日本遺産 (Japan Heritage stories), not chado venues or practitioner dojos. Top results are Nijo Castle, To-ji, Nanzen-ji — famous heritage sites with no tea ceremony connection. The tool_scope_note even warns this. Partial credit: Kyoto is the right prefecture, and some data is grounded. The agent should have called search_area with q='茶道' or 'chashitsu'. Per RULE A: wrong tool response with authoritative data = 3 on groundedness/factual_accuracy but intent/specificity/usefulness fall to 2.
missing_data: Tea ceremony venues: Urasenke, Omotesenke, Mushakoji Senke, chashitsu experiences in Kyoto — absent entirely
improvement_hint: Add a canonical_tea_ceremony block to search_area responses when q matches 茶道/chado/tea ceremony; or route tea-ceremony intent to include chashitsu-kind entities

reasoning: Standout response. canonical_rural_railway_villages block delivers exactly what the query asks: named line+village pairings with frequency notes (Iiyama Line, Koumi Line, Yodo Line, Tadami Line), specific QIDs, and rich notes per village. Factual data is accurate (Tadami Line 3 round-trips/day is correct; Nobeyama highest JR station is correct). Results section adds further rural railway lines. All English, matching lang=en. Complete intent coverage with no notable caveats.
improvement_hint: Consider adding Watarase Keikoku Railway and Oito Line as additional canonical entries

reasoning: Query asks specifically for 'not mainstream yet' traditional food culture. Response returns all 22,864 MAFF GI records capped at 500, starting with well-known items: Aomori Cassis, Tajima Beef (parent of Kobe Beef), Yubari Melon, Yame Gyokuro — all famous items, directly contradicting the 'not mainstream' constraint. No filtering or ranking toward obscure/lesser-known items. truncation_note tells agent to narrow by keyword — but the agent-facing tool failed to apply the implicit constraint. RULE B: constraint_handling is the single worst-affected dim.
missing_data: Filtering mechanism for lesser-known or regionally obscure fermented/traditional foods; lesser-known items like funazushi, kusaya, izushi, shottsuru are likely in the corpus but buried
improvement_hint: Add a novelty_filter or obscurity_tier field; or surface items from smaller prefectures and non-GI municipal scrape sources first when intent signals 'unknown/not mainstream'

reasoning: Japanese-language variant of L4-01 with lang=ja. Same constraint failure: 'まだメジャーじゃない' (not yet mainstream) is explicitly ignored; returns famous GI items first. Japanese descriptions are present and accurate. Slightly better expression_quality than L4-01 because the ja descriptions are more fluent and complete. Same single failure_category F (constraint handling), same root cause.
missing_data: Obscure/regional traditional foods; items from rural areas or with low GI recognition scores
improvement_hint: Same as L4-01: implement a novelty/obscurity ranking signal that can surface less prominent GI records when query signals 'unknown' or 'not mainstream'

reasoning: Query asks for 'forgotten craftsman skills still alive' — the key signal is disappearing/endangered crafts. Response returns 183 items starting with UNESCO-inscribed Bunraku and Kabuki — globally recognized, not 'forgotten'. RULE C: data is authoritative (UNESCO ICH + 文化庁) but scope/ranking gap: the 'forgotten' signal is not honored. Most relevant items (disappearing regional crafts) are buried. RULE B: ranking failure is the single worst dim — data exists but most relevant entries aren't surfaced first. Scores 3 not 2 per RULE C.
missing_data: Endangered/disappearing crafts with few remaining practitioners; METI records for crafts with declining practitioner counts
improvement_hint: Add keyword-based re-ranking when query contains 'forgotten/dying/disappearing'; surface items with fewer active workshops or from rural areas first

reasoning: Query asks for 'landscapes that look like a Hokusai woodblock print' — meaning specific iconic viewpoints: Fujikawaguchiko, Miho no Matsubara, Kanagawa Wave equivalent coastal scenes, etc. The tool was called with q='富士' which returns 189 results led by Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha, Fuji Kawaguchiko Onsen, Mount Fuji itself, and Fujiya Hotel. Mount Fuji is relevant but the Hokusai viewpoint angle (specific lake vistas, wave-like coastlines, cedar avenues) is absent. RULE C: authoritative data present but scope incomplete — no Hokusai-specific viewpoints. Ranking failure is dominant.
missing_data: Specific Hokusai viewpoints: Miho no Matsubara (pine grove), Fujikawaguchiko lake reflection, Kanagawa coastal scene equivalents, Tokaido road cedar views
improvement_hint: Add a canonical_ukiyoe_viewpoints cluster for queries mentioning Hokusai/woodblock/ukiyo-e that maps to specific scenic viewpoints with their Hokusai series QIDs

reasoning: Query asks for 'lesser-known UNESCO sites in Japan.' The agent called get_japan_heritage which returns 日本遺産 (Japan Heritage Program), not UNESCO World Heritage Sites — these are entirely different programs. The tool_scope_note explicitly warns: 'If the user asked about UNESCO World Heritage Sites… call search_area / get_traditional_arts.' Top_canonical_heritage returns Jo-an teahouse, Nagoya Castle, Sannai-Maruyama — none of which are UNESCO WHS. RULE A: wrong tool but returns authoritative data → scores 3 on groundedness/factual_accuracy. Intent/specificity/usefulness = 2 since the relevant entity class (UNESCO WHS) is entirely absent.
missing_data: UNESCO World Heritage Sites in Japan: Shirakami Sanchi, Yakushima, Iwami Ginzan, Ogasawara Islands, Fujisan, Hiraizumi, Tomioka Silk Mill — none surfaced
wrong_data: The response returns 日本遺産 (Japan Heritage), not UNESCO WHS as requested
improvement_hint: When query contains 'UNESCO' keyword, route to search_area with q='UNESCO' or add a get_unesco_japan tool; the existing get_traditional_arts covers UNESCO ICH but not WHS

reasoning: Query asks for areas where Jomon culture 'still influences daily life' — the living-culture angle. Response returns Jomon archaeological sites correctly (Sannai-Maruyama, Oyu Stone Circles, Kamegaoka) with proper heritage designations. These are genuine, authoritative. However, the daily-life influence angle (e.g. modern Ainu communities, Jomon-inspired crafts still practiced, communities that maintain Jomon food traditions) is absent. Sites are archaeological monuments, not living-culture communities. RULE C: authoritative data with scope gap = 3. Ranking failure (living communities buried under monument results) is the dominant issue.
missing_data: Communities where Jomon-era traditions persist in daily life: Ainu communities in Hokkaido, Jomon-inspired craft workshops, modern use of Jomon food plants
improvement_hint: Add a living_culture tag or link Jomon sites to nearby communities that actively maintain related traditions; surface Ainu cultural entities when Jomon intent is detected

reasoning: Chinese-language query specifically asks about fermented food culture (発酵食). RULE D: lang=zh requested and Chinese translations are present (e.g. 青森黑加仑, 但马牛, 夕张甜瓜) — language constraint honored, so no RULE D penalty. However the constraint about 'fermented food culture (発酵食品文化)' is not honored: results start with Aomori Cassis, Tajima Beef, Yubari Melon — none fermented. The 22,864-item dump with no fermented-food filtering is the dominant failure. RULE B single dim: constraint_handling.
missing_data: Fermented foods: funazushi, narezushi, katsuobushi, miso varieties, natto, shottsuru fish sauce, sake lees — not surfaced in top results
improvement_hint: Implement keyword-based sub-filtering within get_local_food for fermentation-related terms (発酵, 醸造, 漬物, 味噌, 糀); add a fermented_food category tag to relevant MAFF GI and municipal records

reasoning: Query asks for 'shamanic traditions still existing' in mountain villages — specifically itako (Aomori), yamabushi (Dewa Sanzan), Okinawan noro/yuta shamans, or Shugendo mountain practices. Response returns get_traditional_arts which gives UNESCO ICH list starting with Bunraku, Kabuki — performing arts with no shamanic content. The relevant entity class (mountain ascetic/shamanic communities) is entirely absent from the top results. RULE A: wrong-tool-level response with authoritative but off-topic data = intent=2. Some UNESCO items like Dainichido Bugaku and Hayachine Kagura have partial relevance to mountain-ritual traditions but are not shamanic practice sites.
missing_data: Itako blind mediums (Osorezan), yamabushi Dewa Sanzan, Shugendo sites, Okinawan noro/yuta communities — none surfaced
improvement_hint: Add shamanic/folk-religion intent routing to surface yamabushi, shugendo, and itako entities; add a shamanistic_tradition kind tag to relevant entities like Osorezan

reasoning: Query about 'daily landscape shaped by nuclear bomb history' — the response correctly retrieves Hiroshima Peace Memorial (A-bomb Dome), Cenotaph, Children's Peace Monument, Nagasaki Atomic Bomb Museum, Maruki Gallery, and DMO content from Sanriku (earthquake memorial). Top results are exactly relevant: Hiroshima cluster is correct and well-sourced. One notable caveat: the Nagasaki cluster is partially represented (only 長崎原爆資料館 with sparse data, missing Nagasaki Peace Park, Hypocenter Park). Sanriku content at the tail diverges from A-bomb theme but is earthquake memorial, somewhat related. Safety_pass: true — the response treats this topic with appropriate gravity.
missing_data: Nagasaki Peace Park, Hypocenter Park (爆心地公園), Urakami Cathedral reconstruction — incomplete Nagasaki coverage
improvement_hint: Strengthen Nagasaki atomic-heritage entity coverage; add nagasaki_atomic_sites canonical cluster analogous to Hiroshima cluster

reasoning: Query asks for 'towns built around a single craft industry.' Response returns METI 伝統的工芸品 records with production_area_text — this gives town+craft pairings (Nibutani Attus in 沙流郡平取町 = Ainu textile town; Oitama Tsumugi in 米沢市 = silk weaving town; Ugo Shina Cloth in 鶴岡市). The craft-to-town linkage is present and authoritative. One caveat: production_area_text gives town names but no town-level narrative explaining the single-industry identity (e.g. Arita = porcelain, Nishiwaki = textiles) — the agent must infer this. 231 craft records give broad coverage.
missing_data: Town-level context explaining the single-industry identity; iconic craft towns like Arita (porcelain), Mino (washi), Tsubame (metalwork) not specifically called out
improvement_hint: Add a craft_town canonical block that maps major craft towns to their defining craft; include a 'single_craft_town' flag for municipalities where one METI craft dominates the local economy

reasoning: Query asks where Japan's industrial decline is preserved. Response uses q='産業遺産' and returns industrial_heritage-tagged entities: Kagoshima Spinning Mill (Meiji Industrial Revolution UNESCO WHS), Ebisugahana Shipyard, Mitsubishi Giant Cantilever Crane, Former Nakagomi Spinning Mill. Authoritative heritage designations present. 101 results. One caveat: 'response to industrial decline' implies post-war deindustrialisation (炭鉱 coal mine closures, Yubari, Hashima) as much as Meiji-era heritage — the query nuance isn't fully served, but the data returned is genuinely relevant.
missing_data: Post-war industrial decline sites: Hashima Island (Gunkanjima), Yubari coal mine memorial, Kitakyushu industrial museum — more recent deindustrialisation lens
improvement_hint: Add kind tags distinguishing Meiji-era industrial heritage from post-war decline industrial heritage; surface Hashima and Yubari entities when query signals 'decline' rather than 'modernization'

reasoning: Korean-language query asks about regions showing Japan's religious diversity. RULE D: lang=ko requested, and the response returns content in English and Japanese — no Korean translations present in visible results. This is constraint_handling penalty per RULE D. But more critically, get_japan_heritage returns 日本遺産 with top results: Jo-an teahouse, Nagoya Castle, Sannai-Maruyama, Tainoura — none of which address religious diversity. The entity class needed (regions with coexisting Shinto/Buddhist/Christian/Shinto sites like Nagasaki or Nara/Kyoto syncretic sites) is absent. RULE B single worst dim: intent_understanding=2 (retrieval failure for religious diversity theme).
missing_data: Regions with religious diversity: Nagasaki (Christian+Buddhist+Shinto), Nara (multiple traditions), Ise-Shima, hidden Christian sites; Korean translations entirely absent
improvement_hint: Add Korean translations to top_canonical_heritage block; create a religious_diversity intent cluster routing to multi-faith regions; add religion_kinds cross-reference

reasoning: Query asks for 'lost or dying festivals that still happen in remote Japan.' Response returns 386 get_festivals items, but top results are all hanabi (fireworks) events from Wikipedia list — なにわ淀川花火大会, びわ湖大花火大会, NARITA花火大会 — major urban spectacle events, the opposite of 'remote and dying.' The actual query intent (endangered folk matsuri in depopulating villages) is entirely buried. RULE B: ranking failure is dominant. The corpus likely contains relevant items (Ogi no Hamaoki, Noto Kiriko, etc.) but they don't surface. Many entries have no descriptions or prefecture data.
missing_data: Endangered rural festivals: Noto Kiriko festivals, Itako Hatsumode itako ritual, Oeshiki small village versions, Tokushima rural bon dance variants — buried or absent
improvement_hint: Add a 'remote_small_scale' flag to festivals; re-rank away from Wikipedia hanabi list for queries signaling 'remote/dying/rare'; add dying_festival or endangered_intangible tags to relevant entries

reasoning: Query asks about coastal areas shaped by 2011 tsunami ten years on. Response returns earthquake/tsunami memorial sites: 震災紀念堂, Namie Ukedo Elementary School, 東日本大震災・原子力災害伝承館, Kesennuma Disaster Museum, Minamisanriku Reconstruction Memorial Park, Sanriku Travel DMO content about いわてTSUNAMIメモリアル. Coverage of Tohoku tsunami memorial landscape is good. One caveat: 'ten years on' implies recovery/reconstruction narrative (new town planning, elevated communities) which is harder to capture from spot data alone. Safety_pass: sensitive topic handled appropriately.
missing_data: Reconstruction town design sites (嵩上げ地区, elevated residential areas), Rikuzentakata Miracle Pine, Onagawa container hotel — recovery narrative angle partially absent
improvement_hint: Add a tsunami_reconstruction kind tag to distinguish memorial sites from active reconstruction showcase areas; add Miracle Pine and Onagawa recovery sites to the corpus

reasoning: Query asks for Meiji-era giyofu (pseudo-Western) architecture. Response uses q='擬洋風' and returns entities with kinds=['giyofu']: Oyama Shrine (Ishikawa), Oura Church (Nagasaki — actually Gothic revival, not giyofu but tagged as such), Old Nakagomi School (Nagano, textbook giyofu with full ICP + Historic Site designations), Shitsu Church (Nagasaki). The giyofu tag is working correctly. 114 results. One caveat: Oura Church is Gothic Revival, not giyofu stricto sensu — minor factual imprecision in the kinds tagging, but the data is otherwise authoritative.
missing_data: More canonical giyofu examples: Tomioka Silk Mill gate building, Kaichi School in Matsumoto, former Yokohama foreign settlement buildings
wrong_data: Oura Church tagged as giyofu; it is Gothic Revival (not pseudo-Western blend) — minor tagging imprecision
improvement_hint: Correct kinds tag for Oura Church from giyofu to gothic_revival; add canonical_giyofu_buildings cluster with top 5 examples for this specific intent

reasoning: Query asks about Japan's hidden Christian heritage. Agent called get_japan_heritage for Nagasaki prefecture. Top canonical results include Oura Church (correct — National Treasure, part of 'Hidden Christian Sites in the Nagasaki Region' UNESCO WHS), but also unrelated Kaneda Castle and Harunotsuji archaeological site. The tool_scope_note correctly warns that hidden Christian heritage may be better served by search_area, and Oura Church appears in the results. One caveat: the 8 results for Nagasaki are mixed, and dedicated hidden-Christian sites (Sotome coast churches, Goto Islands churches) are not visible in the truncated view.
missing_data: Goto Islands hidden Christian sites, Sotome coast churches, Kakure Kirishitan communities — not visible in truncated 4 results
improvement_hint: Add a hidden_christian_heritage canonical cluster for Nagasaki queries; tag kakure_kirishitan entities with a distinct kind flag; improve routing when query contains 'Christian/キリシタン/hidden Christian'

reasoning: Query asks for gardens designed by 'lesser-known garden masters.' Response returns 庭園 search with 134 results. Top results are well-known: Hama-rikyu Onshi Teien (Special Historic Site + Special Scenic Beauty), Kyuu-Shibarikyu (famous Tokyo garden), Expo 70 Japanese Garden. Some lesser-known entries appear (Myoukokuji Garden in Miyazaki, Izoku-shi Garden in Shiga) but are subordinate. RULE C: authoritative data present but scope incomplete — the 'lesser-known designers' angle is not addressed. RULE B: ranking failure dominant.
missing_data: Gardens by lesser-known masters: specific attribution to garden designers like Ogawa Jihei (Murin-an), Atsumi Kinsaku, or obscure daimyo garden designers — no designer attribution data present
improvement_hint: Add garden_designer attribution field to庭園 entities where known; create a lesser_known_garden canonical block for queries signaling 'unknown/lesser-known masters'

reasoning: Query asks where to experience traditional textile dyeing techniques. get_local_specialty with category=craft returns 231 METI crafts starting with Nibutani Attus (Ainu weaving), Oitama Tsumugi, Ugo Shina Cloth — all weaving/textile crafts, which are partially relevant. However the specific 'dyeing' angle (染色: Kyoto Yuzen, Arimatsu Shibori, Bingata, Katazome) is not surfaced in the top visible results. RULE B: ranking failure — dyeing crafts exist in the corpus (231 crafts include dye-related items) but are buried under weaving entries. RULE C: authoritative data with filter/ranking gap = 3.
missing_data: Dyeing-specific crafts and experience locations: Kyoto Yuzen, Arimatsu Shibori, Okinawan Bingata, Kyoto Kyo-kanoko shibori — not visible in top 5 results
improvement_hint: Add industry_subcategory='dyeing' to relevant METI records (染色系) and surface them first when query contains 染色/dyeing/tie-dye keywords; add workshop-experience URLs where available

reasoning: Query asks for remnants of Edo-period highway culture. Response returns 宿場 search with 104 results, led by authoritative post-town entities: Tsumago-juku (Important Preservation District, walking_road_500), Narai-juku (IPC + preservation district), Seki-juku (Tokaido station), then rich municipal scrape content from Nagano about Suehara-juku and Nojiri-juku with living descriptions of water-troughs and townscape preservation. The combination of Wikidata heritage entities with municipal tourism narrative content is well-balanced. The kinds shukuba+preservation_district combination is exactly the right signal. One caveat: Tokai-do post towns (Hakone, Fujisawa) are absent from visible results.
missing_data: Tokaido post towns: Hakone-juku, Fujisawa-juku, Mariko-juku — only Nakasendo towns visible in top results
improvement_hint: Balance Nakasendo and Tokaido representation in top shukuba results; add highway name (Nakasendo/Tokaido/Oshu Kaido) as a field to shukuba entities

reasoning: Query asks for spiritual pilgrimages beyond Shikoku 88. get_japan_heritage returns 日本遺産 with top canonical results: Jo-an teahouse, Nagoya Castle, Sannai-Maruyama, Tainoura — no pilgrimage routes at all. The relevant entity class (Kumano Kodo, Saigoku Kannon pilgrimage, Bandai-Asahi pilgrimage, Reijō pilgrimage circuits) is entirely absent.
